section.title: Songs of Thanksgiving
section.sectionRationale: This brief doxology closes the entire Immanuel section that began in chapter 7, turning the promises of the Branch and the regathered remnant into a hymn the redeemed will actually sing: anger turned to comfort, God become salvation, and joyful water drawn from the wells of deliverance. It functions like the Song of the Sea after the exodus, a fitting hymnic seal on a section built around a second exodus.
section.thesis: On the day the promised King reigns and the remnant is gathered, God's people will sing that His anger has turned to comfort, that He Himself has become their salvation, and they will proclaim His name to all the earth with joy drawn from the wells of deliverance.
section.frameNote: Two short songs: a personal declaration that God's anger has turned to comfort and He has become salvation; and a corporate summons to draw water joyfully from the wells of salvation and declare God's name among the nations.

verses.text:
In that day you will say: 
“O LORD, I will praise You. Although You were angry with me, Your anger has turned away, and You have comforted me.
Surely God is my {{yeshuah|salvation}}; I will trust and not be afraid. For the LORD GOD is my strength and my song, and He also has become my salvation.”

units.label: The second song
units.note: wells of salvation, declared among the nations

verses.text: With joy you will draw water from the springs of {{yeshuah2|salvation}},

and on that day you will say: 
“Give praise to the LORD; proclaim His name! Make His works known among the peoples; declare that His name is exalted.
Sing to the LORD, for He has done glorious things. Let this be known in all the earth.
Cry out and sing, O citizen of Zion, for great among you is the {{qadosh4|Holy One}} of Israel.”

yeshuah.gloss: Salvation, deliverance, rescue, from the same root that gives us the name Yeshua, Jesus. Twice in verse 2 this word appears, God is my yeshuah and has become my yeshuah, directly anticipating the name given to Mary's son, whose very name means the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21.

yeshuah2.gloss: Salvation, deliverance. The wells or springs of yeshuah, salvation, from which the redeemed draw water with joy, an image later drawn on by Jesus Himself in John 4:14 and John 7:37-38, offering living water that satisfies forever.

qadosh4.gloss: Holy, set apart. The Holy One of Israel closes this brief hymn just as it opened the whole book's indictment in chapter 1, showing that the same holiness that once exposed Judah's sin is now the very reason for their praise once redemption is accomplished.

basic.context: This short chapter is a doxology, a song of praise, closing the entire section of oracles that began with Ahaz's crisis in chapter 7. Having promised the Branch, the child-King, and the gathering of the scattered remnant, Isaiah now gives the redeemed people the very words they will sing: God's anger has turned to comfort, He Himself has become their salvation, and they will joyfully proclaim His name to the whole earth.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a genuine, future-oriented song of praise, meant to be sung once the promises of chapters 7-11 are realized, not a description of a past event. Its close relationship to the Song of the Sea in Exodus 15, sung after Israel's first deliverance, deliberately signals that this passage functions as the capstone hymn to the 'second exodus' promised in chapter 11, showing that the whole Immanuel section builds toward worship as its intended, ultimate response.
basic.originalLanguages: Twice in verse 2 the word yeshuah, salvation, appears, God is my yeshuah, He has become my yeshuah, the very root behind the name Yeshua, Jesus, given because He would save His people from their sins. The redeemed will draw water joyfully from the wells of yeshuah, salvation, and will declare that the qadosh, Holy One, of Israel is great among them, the very title that once introduced Judah's indictment now becoming the reason for their praise.
basic.typology: The wells of salvation from which the redeemed draw water joyfully anticipate Jesus's own offer of living water in John 4:14 and John 7:37-38, water that satisfies eternally rather than temporarily. The turning of God's anger into comfort previews the full reconciliation accomplished at the cross, where wrath is satisfied and comfort flows freely to those who trust in Christ.
basic.christ: The name yeshuah, salvation, used twice of God Himself in this song, is the very root of the name given to Mary's son, Jesus, whose name literally means the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21, so that this hymn's declaration, God has become my salvation, finds its ultimate, personal fulfillment in the person of Christ. Jesus later draws directly on this chapter's image of wells of salvation when He offers living water that becomes in believers a spring welling up to eternal life, John 4:14, and invites the thirsty to come and drink, John 7:37-38.
basic.god: God's anger, real and justly provoked by sin, is shown here to be turned away, replaced by comfort, once His redemptive purposes are accomplished, proving that His wrath is never His final or ultimate disposition toward those He redeems. He is not merely the giver of salvation but is Himself the salvation His people receive, strength and song wrapped into a single, personal gift.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 15:1-2 provides the model for this song, celebrating God as strength and song after Israel's first deliverance through the sea, language this chapter deliberately echoes. Matthew 1:21 explains that Jesus's name means the LORD saves, directly rooted in the same Hebrew word, yeshuah, used twice in this chapter. John 4:13-14 has Jesus offer living water that becomes an eternal spring in believers. John 7:37-38 has Jesus invite the thirsty to come to Him and drink, echoing this chapter's wells of salvation.
basic.oneStory: This brief hymn shows the intended end of the whole redemptive story: not merely deliverance accomplished, but a redeemed people singing that God Himself has become their salvation. That salvation, yeshuah, finds its personal name and full accomplishment in Jesus, whose name means exactly what this song declares, and whose living water finally and forever satisfies the thirst these ancient words anticipated.

context.literary: This short psalm functions as the doxological conclusion to the entire block of Immanuel oracles running from chapter 7 through chapter 11, deliberately structured as two brief songs, each introduced by 'in that day you will say,' moving from personal, individual praise (verses 1-2) to corporate, missionary proclamation (verses 4-6). Its language and structure closely echo the Song of the Sea in Exodus 15, reinforcing the 'second exodus' theme just concluded in chapter 11.
context.historical: Rather than describing a specific historical event, this chapter anticipates a future celebration once the promises of the preceding oracles, the child-King's reign, the felling of Assyria's pride, and the gathering of the remnant, are realized; it functions less as a historical report and more as liturgy prepared in advance for a hoped-for future.
context.canonical: As a short thanksgiving psalm embedded within prophetic material, this chapter parallels other Old Testament victory songs, Exodus 15, Judges 5, 1 Samuel 2, and its language of drawing water joyfully from wells of salvation becomes directly significant in the Gospel of John's development of Jesus as the source of living water.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide the redeemed community with the actual words of praise they will one day sing once God's promised salvation through the Branch and the gathered remnant is realized, closing the Immanuel section with worship rather than mere prediction.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a prophetic song anticipating future praise rather than a prescription for a specific liturgical formula that must be used verbatim. The enduring principle is that genuine encounter with God's salvation should produce exactly this kind of response: personal trust replacing fear, and corporate proclamation of God's greatness to the nations.

debates.question: Is 'that day' in this chapter the same near-term day as the fall of Assyria and Ahaz's crisis, or does it point toward the ultimate messianic and eschatological fulfillment described in chapter 11?

views.label: Primarily the same near horizon as the surrounding oracles
views.case: The phrase 'in that day' throughout chapters 7-12 has often referred to the more immediate resolution of the Assyrian crisis, so this song may function as the anticipated response to that specific, nearer deliverance.

views.label: The ultimate, messianic and eschatological horizon of chapter 11
views.case: Chapter 12 follows immediately after chapter 11's full description of the Messiah's reign and the second exodus gathering of the whole remnant, suggesting this song responds to that fuller, more ultimate fulfillment rather than merely the Assyrian crisis alone.

debates.bearing: Given Isaiah's consistent pattern of near and far fulfillment throughout this section, the song likely functions on both levels: a genuine, appropriate response to any real deliverance God grants His people now, while pointing toward and anticipating the fullest, final response of praise when Christ's kingdom is completely realized.

study.originalLanguages: The word yeshuah, salvation, appears twice in verse 2 alone, God is my yeshuah, He has become my yeshuah, the very root behind the name Yeshua, Jesus, given because He would save His people from their sins. The redeemed draw water joyfully from the wells of yeshuah, and declare that the qadosh, Holy One, of Israel, the very title that opened Judah's indictment in chapter 1, is now great among them and worthy of praise.

typology.type: The wells of salvation from which the redeemed draw water joyfully, Isaiah 12:3
typology.fulfillment: Jesus's offer of living water that becomes an eternal spring in believers, John 4:14, and His invitation to the thirsty to come and drink, John 7:37-38.

typology.type: God's anger turned to comfort, Isaiah 12:1
typology.fulfillment: The full reconciliation accomplished at the cross, where God's wrath against sin is satisfied and comfort flows freely to those in Christ.

study.christ: The word yeshuah, salvation, declared twice over in this brief song, is the very root of the name given to Mary's son, Jesus, whose name literally means the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21, so this hymn's central confession, God has become my salvation, finds its personal, historical fulfillment in Christ Himself. Jesus later draws directly on this chapter's image of joyfully drawn wells of salvation, offering living water that becomes in believers a spring welling up to eternal life, John 4:14, and inviting all who thirst to come to Him and drink, John 7:37-38.
study.god: God's anger, real and justly provoked, is shown here to give way to comfort once His redemptive purposes are accomplished, revealing that wrath is never His final or ultimate disposition toward a redeemed and repentant people. He is not merely the giver of salvation from a distance but is Himself the salvation His people receive personally, becoming their strength and their song.

crossReferences.note: Provides the model for this song, celebrating God as strength and song after Israel's first great deliverance through the sea.
Explains that Jesus's name means the LORD saves, directly rooted in the same Hebrew word, yeshuah, sung twice in this chapter.
Jesus offers living water that becomes an eternal spring welling up within believers.
Jesus invites the thirsty to come to Him and drink, directly echoing this chapter's wells of salvation.

study.oneStory: This brief hymn reveals the intended end of the whole redemptive story: not merely deliverance accomplished but a redeemed people singing that God Himself has become their salvation. That salvation, yeshuah, receives its personal name and full accomplishment in Jesus, whose name means precisely what this song declares, and whose living water finally and forever satisfies the thirst these ancient words first anticipated.

quiz.q: What transformation does the first song in verses 1-2 celebrate?

options.text: God's anger has turned away and become comfort, and He Himself has become the singer's salvation.
options.note: This is the heart of the song, a personal declaration of reconciliation and trust.

options.text: The complete destruction of all Judah's enemies with no mention of God's own disposition.
options.note: The song centers specifically on the change in God's own disposition toward His people, anger turned to comfort.

options.text: A military victory won through human strength.
options.note: The song attributes salvation entirely to God, not human military achievement.

options.text: The restoration of the temple's physical structure.
options.note: The song's focus is personal and relational, God's turned anger and salvation, not physical temple restoration specifically.

quiz.q: What is significant about the Hebrew word yeshuah, salvation, appearing twice in verse 2?

options.text: It is the very root of the name Jesus, whose name means the LORD saves.
options.note: Matthew 1:21 explains Jesus's name means exactly this, directly connecting this ancient hymn to Christ.

options.text: It is an unrelated word with no connection to any New Testament name.
options.note: Yeshuah is directly the root of Yeshua, Jesus's Hebrew name.

options.text: It refers only to physical healing from illness.
options.note: The term carries the broader sense of deliverance and rescue, not merely physical healing.

options.text: It is used only once, not twice, in this passage.
options.note: The word yeshuah appears twice in verse 2 alone, both times describing God Himself as salvation.

quiz.q: What does the second song, verses 3-6, call the redeemed people to do?

options.text: Draw water joyfully from the wells of salvation and proclaim God's name and works to all the earth.
options.note: The song moves from personal praise to corporate, outward-facing proclamation to the nations.

options.text: Keep their deliverance a private, personal secret.
options.note: The song explicitly calls for making God's works known among the peoples and declaring Him in all the earth.

options.text: Focus exclusively on internal, personal devotion with no missionary dimension.
options.note: The song explicitly has a global, missionary dimension, declaring God's name among the peoples.

options.text: Mourn their past sins rather than celebrate deliverance.
options.note: The tone throughout is joyful praise and proclamation, not ongoing mourning.

quiz.q: How does this chapter's imagery connect to Jesus's ministry in the Gospel of John?

options.text: Jesus directly echoes the image of wells of salvation, offering living water that satisfies eternally.
options.note: John 4:14 and John 7:37-38 both draw on exactly this kind of water-and-salvation imagery.

options.text: There is no connection between this chapter and John's Gospel.
options.note: Jesus's living water discourses closely parallel this chapter's wells-of-salvation imagery.

options.text: Jesus rejects the imagery of water as a symbol of spiritual life.
options.note: Jesus embraces and develops this very imagery rather than rejecting it.

options.text: The connection is only to Jesus's crucifixion, not His teaching ministry.
options.note: The clearest connections are to Jesus's teaching about living water in John 4 and 7, not specifically the crucifixion narrative.

quiz.q: What earlier biblical song does Isaiah 12 most closely echo in form and theme?

options.text: The Song of the Sea in Exodus 15, celebrating God as strength and song after Israel's first great deliverance.
options.note: Both songs share the language of God as 'my strength and my song' and celebrate deliverance through water imagery.

options.text: A song found nowhere else in Scripture.
options.note: The parallels to Exodus 15 are widely recognized by readers of Isaiah.

options.text: The book of Lamentations.
options.note: Lamentations is a mourning text, the opposite tone from this joyful thanksgiving song.

options.text: The curses of Deuteronomy 28.
options.note: Isaiah 12 is a song of blessing and praise, not a list of covenant curses.

questions: This song celebrates God's anger turning to comfort. Is there an area of your life where you still feel God's disapproval rather than resting in the comfort His redemption offers?

The singer declares, 'He also has become my salvation,' not merely that God gives salvation from a distance. How would your daily trust change if you truly believed God Himself, not just His gifts, is your salvation?
The redeemed are called to declare God's works among the peoples, not keep their joy private. Who in your life needs to hear what God has done for you?

The image of drawing water joyfully from wells of salvation anticipates Jesus's offer of living water. Where are you currently trying to draw satisfaction from a well that will leave you thirsty again?

This whole song functions as the intended response to everything promised in the preceding chapters. Does your own life currently include regular, genuine praise in response to what God has done, or has it become mostly requests and complaints?

translationNotes.note: 'In that day' is a recurring phrase throughout chapters 7-12 marking a decisive future moment, here marking the fitting response of praise once the preceding promises are realized.
'The LORD GOD is my strength and my song' closely echoes Exodus 15:2 almost word for word, deliberately tying this hymn to the original song sung after Israel's exodus deliverance.

'Springs of salvation' uses plural 'wells' or 'springs,' possibly evoking the ceremonial water-drawing rituals later associated with the Feast of Tabernacles, the very festival during which Jesus stood and offered living water in John 7:37-38.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from the Makor scripture toolkit for Isaiah 12:1-6.
